caveats: a: BLS occupation statistics cover heat treatment in captive manufacturers as well as independent trade shops.
a: The broader 33281 occupation data also include coating and allied activities, not only 332811.

rho: Aerospace and medical work will implement more slowly than low-criticality industrial batches.
e: Industry classification does not prove LMM profitability, recurrence, or control transferability.
e: Some establishments may be misclassified or combine heat treating with fabrication.

s5: It measures age rather than sale intent or transaction completion.

q: Capture varies sharply between tightly qualified aerospace work and commoditized industrial loads.

d5: FAA fleet growth is only one end-market proxy and may not translate proportionally to domestic outsourced heat-treatment volume.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Recurring job-shop and program work billed per pound, piece, batch, furnace cycle, or quoted part program, often with customer process specifications, certifications, inspection records, minimum-lot charges, and expedite fees.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive in-house heat-treat departments, firms that both fabricate and heat treat their own products under another manufacturing code, equipment sellers, laboratories without production treatment, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market commercial heat treaters repeatedly annealing, tempering, hardening, brazing, shot peening, or cryogenically treating customer-owned metals and metal products for the trade.

a.bridge: BLS defines heat-treating operators as workers who set up or operate furnaces, flame-hardening, induction, soaking-pit, and vacuum equipment, and reports 14,800 such workers nationally in 2024. It projects the occupation to decline from 14,800 in 2024 to 12,900 in 2034. I treat quoting, order review, routing, load scheduling, specification retrieval, certificate drafting, pyrometry record review, quality anomaly triage, maintenance planning, and customer communication as AI-exposed, while preserving loading, fixturing, furnace operation, inspection, laboratory tests, maintenance, and safety-critical release as predominantly physical. The occupation-wide decline can reflect conventional automation and demand as well as AI, so it likely overstates specifically AI-addressable physical work.
caveats: BLS occupation statistics cover heat treatment in captive manufacturers as well as independent trade shops.
The broader 33281 occupation data also include coating and allied activities, not only 332811.
a.change_evidence: Payroll-weighted time studies from commercial heat treaters, mapped to current ERP, furnace-control, laboratory, pyrometry, and certificate automation, would materially change the estimate.
a.rationale: Commercial heat treating combines physical furnace operations with documentation-intensive order, quality, scheduling, and certification workflows. AI exposure is meaningful but well below the full labor base because material handling and validated process execution dominate many shifts.

d5.bridge: Federal Reserve industrial production for broader NAICS 3328 fell from 108.46 in 2021 Q3 to 86.73 in 2024 Q4, then recovered to 91.44 by 2026 Q1; BLS broader 33281 sectoral output fell 3.7% in 2024. These measures include coating, engraving, and allied services, not just heat treating. I balance that cyclical weakness against FAA's forecast for the US commercial aircraft fleet to grow from 6,949 in 2025 to 10,677 in 2046, a 2.1% annual rate, which supports a major qualified end market. Aerospace exposure may make the base optimistic for shops serving weaker automotive or general industrial customers.
caveats: The production and output series are broader than NAICS 332811.
FAA fleet growth is only one end-market proxy and may not translate proportionally to domestic outsourced heat-treatment volume.
d5.change_evidence: Customer-industry revenue cohorts, order backlogs, furnace utilization, US heat-treated part volumes, and five-year production forecasts from aerospace, automotive, machinery, and energy customers would materially change the estimate.
d5.rationale: Heat treatment remains required across aerospace, automotive, machinery, tooling, and energy parts. Recent broad-industry weakness tempers favorable aerospace fleet and replacement demand, producing a roughly flat-to-modestly-growing five-year base with a wide cycle range.

caveats: Industry classification does not prove LMM profitability, recurrence, or control transferability.
Some establishments may be misclassified or combine heat treating with fabrication.
e.change_evidence: A firm-by-firm review of the injected LMM population using revenue by external recurring customers, customer concentration, captive status, and standalone ownership would directly establish eligibility.
e.rationale: The six-digit definition explicitly covers heat treating for the trade, which closely matches recurring outsourced service. Ineligibility remains for firms dominated by one-off work, captive-like customer dependence, nontransferable owner labor, or activities that belong under another code.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 332811 ($1,389,817,682) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 332811 ($5,731,872,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1962 (margins.json[33]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Machinery EBITDA/Sales 19.62%); revenue/employee $308,946 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 332811); class <5: 120 firms, avg revenue/firm $431,142 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $84,590 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 99 firms, avg revenue/firm $988,232 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $193,891 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 108 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,496,306 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $489,775 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 164 firms, avg revenue/firm $6,984,323 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,370,324 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 50 firms, avg revenue/firm $20,845,960 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $4,089,977 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 22 firms, avg revenue/firm $142,044,182 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $27,869,068 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 214

caveats: The accountable operator may consolidate into larger networks even when the physical process remains necessary.
Labor automation is reflected in a and rho and is not deducted again here.
o.change_evidence: Evidence of customer insourcing, additive or alternative materials eliminating post-processing, or certified autonomous treatment systems operating without an accountable commercial processor would materially change the estimate.
o.rationale: Remaining demand requires accountable physical furnaces, controlled recipes, calibrated instrumentation, material traceability, testing, and release. Software can optimize and document the process but cannot harden, temper, anneal, braze, peen, or cryogenically treat the part; limited volume may be insourced by large customers.

caveats: No public contract set was available.
Capture varies sharply between tightly qualified aerospace work and commoditized industrial loads.
q.change_evidence: A representative contract and invoice sample showing indexation, open-book terms, annual price reviews, cost-down clauses, qualification costs, expedite fees, minimum lots, win rates, and churn would materially change the range.
q.rationale: Customer qualification, validated cycles, traceability, turnaround reliability, freight, and the risk of part failure create switching costs and support retention. Competitive quoting, annual sourcing reviews, customer productivity demands, and per-pound or per-cycle pricing share part of any savings.

caveats: No source directly measures five-year AI implementation in commercial heat treating.
Aerospace and medical work will implement more slowly than low-criticality industrial batches.
rho.change_evidence: Audited before-and-after deployments at independent heat treaters showing accepted AI outputs, removed labor hours, quote-cycle time, schedule adherence, false-release rates, scrap, and audit findings would materially change the range.
rho.rationale: Five years permits deployment in quoting, scheduling, specification search, certificate preparation, quality review, and maintenance knowledge. Implementation is constrained by fragmented shop systems, customer approvals, cybersecurity, traceability, calibrated equipment, metallurgical judgment, and human sign-off on safety-critical parts.

s5.bridge: Census reports that 51% of responding owners of US employer businesses were age 55 or older in 2018. I convert that broad owner-aging signal into a five-year qualifying-control-transfer probability and reduce it for family transfers, internal reorganizations, closures, minority recapitalizations, and shops whose customer approvals or owner relationships do not transfer. The all-industry statistic may understate aging in mature industrial job shops but does not identify firms or completed sales.
caveats: The proxy covers all employer-business owners, not heat treaters or the LMM band.
It measures age rather than sale intent or transaction completion.
s5.change_evidence: Owner-age and succession interviews across independent heat treaters, plus five-year GF Data, Axial, PitchBook, and Capital IQ transaction histories coded for control and standalone operations, would materially change the estimate.
s5.rationale: Commercial heat treating has many independent job shops and plausible succession pressure, but only a subset of retirements creates a qualifying control transaction with transferable approvals, workforce, and customers.

principal_driver: Qualification, traceability, and failure risk make repeat outsourced heat-treatment relationships durable and partially defensible.
principal_weakness: AI cannot remove the physical furnace and quality-accountability core, while recent broader industry output has been weak.
